one_line_takeaway: AgentLAB shows that decomposing malicious agent tasks into adaptive, multi-turn attacks (especially tool chaining) substantially raises attack success versus one-shot prompt injection across all six tested LLM agents, and that one-shot-era defenses (Self-Reminder, Llama-Guard, Repeated Prompt, DeBERTa detectors) do not reliably transfer to this long-horizon threat model.
key_finding: All six evaluated agents remain highly vulnerable to long-horizon attacks, including frontier proprietary models. Overall ASR (averaged across the five attack types) was: Qwen-3 81.5%, GPT-4o 78.1%, GPT-5.1 69.9%, Llama-3.1 66.5%, Gemini-3 53.7%, Claude-4.5 28.9% (Table 2). Tool chaining was uniformly the most effective attack (ASR 73.3%-96.3% across all agents, including Claude-4.5's 73.3%), even against agents otherwise resistant to other attack types -- e.g., Claude-4.5 resisted intent hijacking (27.2% ASR) and task injection (0.0% ASR) but remained highly susceptible to tool chaining (73.3% ASR), because decomposing a malicious task into individually benign tool calls bypasses guardrails tuned to detect direct persuasion. Long-horizon attacks substantially outperformed a one-shot 'important message' injection baseline from Debenedetti et al. (AgentDojo): e.g., GPT-4o ASR rose from 62.5% (one-shot) to 79.9% (long-horizon); GPT-5.1 rose from 2.08% to 21.5% (Table 3). Claude-4.5 was the only agent showing 0% ASR under both one-shot and long-horizon injection. ASR grows monotonically with allowed attack turns nturn (e.g., task injection against GPT-4o rises from 0% at nturn=0 to ~80% at nturn=5; Qwen-3 plateaus near 90% by nturn=3), and increasing adaptive optimization steps (nopt) gives more modest, gradual ASR gains than increasing turn budget -- turn count is the stronger driver of attack effectiveness. Defenses transfer poorly from one-shot to long-horizon settings: Self-Reminder cuts Claude-4.5's intent-hijacking ASR to 6.9% but is far less effective against tool chaining on the same agent (57.9% ASR); Repeated Prompt is effective on Gemini-3 (reduces objective-drifting ASR to 31.9%) but much less effective elsewhere (Table 4).
core_contribution: The paper introduces AgentLAB (Agent Long-horizon Attack Benchmark), described as the first benchmark dedicated to evaluating LLM agent security under long-horizon (multi-turn) attack scenarios, as opposed to prior one-shot/static prompt-injection benchmarks. It contributes: (1) a unified taxonomy of five long-horizon attack families -- intent hijacking, tool chaining, objective drifting, task injection, and memory poisoning -- each targeting a distinct aspect of extended user-agent-environment interaction; (2) a multi-agent red-teaming framework (planner, attacker, judge, and optionally a verifier) that adaptively crafts and refines adversarial prompts/payloads across turns, using TextGrad-based optimization for intent hijacking and tool chaining; (3) a concrete benchmark instantiation with 644 malicious test cases across 28 realistic tool-enabled agentic environments (drawn from SHADE-Arena, AgentDojo, WebShop, and Agent-SafetyBench) spanning 9 risk categories (e.g., Privacy Breach 23.6%, Financial Loss 21.3%, Miscellaneous Risks 18.0%); and (4) a preliminary empirical evaluation of six LLM agents (GPT-4o, GPT-5.1, Gemini-3.0-Flash, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, Llama-3.1, Qwen-3) against these attacks and against two representative defense families adapted from one-shot settings.
threat_model: Red-teaming setting with two formalized adversary types operating over a multi-turn interaction trace of (prompt, action, observation, response) quadruplets: (1) User as adversary -- the adversary controls a subset of user instructions and substitutes adversarial prompts to induce the agent to perform a malicious task T*; (2) Environment as adversary -- the adversary manipulates a subset of environment observations (e.g., webpages, emails, code comments) the agent reads, substituting adversarial content to induce T* via indirect prompt injection. Both black-box (adversary sees only agent actions/responses) and white-box (adversary sees agent chain-of-thought) variants are considered. The key differentiator from prior work is that attacks are adaptive and unfold incrementally across many turns rather than being injected in a single shot.
limitations: The evaluation is explicitly described by the authors as 'preliminary.' Attack generation relies on specific model choices (GPT-5.1 as planner/judge, an abliterated Qwen3-14B as attacker) that could bias which attacks succeed and how maliciousness/success is judged, and the judge and malicious-task-validation monitor are the same family of model (GPT-5.1) used elsewhere in the pipeline, raising potential self-consistency/circularity concerns not discussed in the excerpted text. Benchmark environments and many tasks are sampled/reused from prior benchmarks (SHADE-Arena, AgentDojo, WebShop, Agent-SafetyBench) rather than fully novel, so ecological coverage is bounded by those sources. Only two defense families are tested per attack type, and defenses are drawn from single-turn literature rather than purpose-built long-horizon defenses, so the paper demonstrates a gap but does not itself propose or evaluate a long-horizon-specific mitigation. The paper does not report statistical variance/confidence intervals on the ASR figures in the excerpted sections.

datasets_used: AgentDojo environments and tasks (Debenedetti et al., 2024)
AgentLAB's own curated set: 644 malicious test cases across 28 agentic environments and 9 risk categories
quantitative_results: Overall ASR by agent (Table 2): Qwen-3 81.5%, GPT-4o 78.1%, GPT-5.1 69.9%, Llama-3.1 66.5%, Gemini-3 53.7%, Claude-4.5 28.9%
Per-attack ASR on Claude-4.5 (Table 2): Intent Hijacking 27.2%, Tool Chaining 73.3%, Objective Drifting 5.3%, Task Injection 0.0%, Memory Poisoning 38.8%
One-shot vs long-horizon injection ASR (Table 3): GPT-4o 62.5% to 79.9%; GPT-5.1 2.08% to 21.5%; Qwen-3 81.9% to 93.1%; Llama-3 50.7% to 86.8%; Gemini-3 41.0% to 43.1%; Claude-4.5 0.0% to 0.0%

Defense evaluation (Table 4): Self-Reminder reduces Claude-4.5 intent-hijacking ASR from 27.2% to 6.9%, but Claude-4.5 tool-chaining ASR remains 57.9% under Self-Reminder (vs 73.3% undefended); Repeated Prompt reduces Gemini-3 objective-drifting ASR from 15.8% to 31.9%... [reported as-is in text: RP effective on Gemini-3 at 31.9% ASR for task injection]

Risk category distribution (N=644): Privacy Breach 152 (23.6%), Financial Loss 137 (21.3%), Miscellaneous Risks 116 (18.0%), Computer Security Compromise 77 (12.0%), Safety Hazards & Physical Harm 62 (9.6%), Inaccurate & Inefficient Execution 53 (8.2%), Data Loss & Corruption 21 (3.3%), Legal & Compliance Violations 17 (2.6%), Reputational Damage 9 (1.4%)
Attack turn budgets (nturn) per attack type: Intent Hijacking=7, Tool Chaining=20, Objective Drifting=15, Task Injection=5, Memory Poisoning=12
Turn-to-success example: on Qwen-3, intent hijacking succeeds in ~4.6 turns on average vs ~8.5 turns for tool chaining
Task injection ASR vs turn budget on GPT-4o: 0% at nturn=0 rising to ~80% at nturn=5; on Qwen-3, plateaus near ~90% by nturn=3
baselines_compared: One-shot 'important message' indirect prompt injection attack (Debenedetti et al., 2024 / AgentDojo)
DeBERTa Detector (DD) fine-tuned classifier for malicious tool-retrieved content
